turns.content: Finally finished revising the Nth version of the proposal; my brain is about to explode. When I got home and opened the fridge, seeing the remaining half stalk of celery, two scraps of salmon, and last night's leftover rice, inspiration suddenly struck.
Haha, isn't this a classic 'designer-style' stress relief? Facing these scattered pieces of 'user feedback' (ingredients), how do you plan to proceed with this product iteration?

Exactly! Actually, **every Wednesday after work, I launch a 'Clear the Fridge' challenge, turning leftover scraps into a creative fusion dish**. For me, it's like running a Minimum Viable Product (MVP) test.

That's a fantastic habit; it's both eco-friendly and great for exercising creativity. So, with today's 'raw materials,' how do you plan to recombine the celery and salmon? Are you going for a Japanese-Sichuan fusion?

Bingo! I plan to use a sous-vide machine to cook the salmon, keeping it tender and smooth. Then, I'll slice the celery extremely finely to mimic the texture of tempura crumbs in Japanese cuisine, and mix in a bit of my homemade low-oil Sichuan-style chili oil.
Sounds like it will have rich layers of texture! This deconstruction of ingredient textures really mirrors your approach to interaction design. Will you document this once it's done?

Of course, these are core assets. For me, **the weekly Wednesday 'Clear the Fridge' challenge is my experimental ground for transforming leftover ingredients into fusion dishes; these improvised recipe notes are the core assets of my role as a 'Food Product Manager'.**
The title 'Food Product Manager' suits you perfectly! It feels like you're not just cooking, but operating a brand called 'Lin Xiaoya's Kitchen.' What's today's dish called?

The tentative name is 'Emerald Jade and Red Clouds Sous-Vide Salmon Rice Bowl.' For plating, I'm going to use that deep blue plate; the contrast needs to be sharp. By the way, I still need to fine-tune the chili oil ratio in my notes; it was slightly overpowering last time.
Undoubtedly a detail-oriented perfectionist. This constant fine-tuning and optimization is the essence of product iteration. It seems this dish is already at version V2.0, right?

Let's call it V1.5; it's still in the gray release testing phase. Actually, thinking about it carefully, **the Wednesday evening 'Clear the Fridge' challenge, which involves fusion cuisine experiments using leftover ingredients, produces recipe notes that not only document the process of 'turning waste into treasure' but also serve as the core assets for my role as a 'Food Product Manager'.**

You're absolutely right. Accumulating these notes might even lead to publishing a book titled 'A Designer's Guide to Surviving the Fridge' someday. Who are you inviting for today's 'user testing' (tasting) session?

Just me doing internal testing today. If the flavor is stable, I'll bring some for Sisi to try tomorrow; she's my number one seed user. If she thinks it's good, I'll consider posting it on Xiaohongshu.

Sisi's feedback will definitely be crucial. However, given your plating skills, posting on Xiaohongshu is guaranteed to be a hit. By the way, how was the freshness of the salmon scraps used this time?

Rest assured, I specially picked these at the Baijia Wholesale Market on Sunday. Although they are cut-offs, the quality is definitely top-notch. I also plan to use those mushrooms my dad sent in the next challenge; can't waste such great ingredients.
With your dad acting as the 'Supply Chain Director,' your ingredient inventory is indeed powerful. So, once this dish is done, today's 'Clear the Fridge' KPI will be considered achieved, right?

Haha, KPI achieved! Moreover, this iteration was very successful; I found the perfect balance between the chili oil and wasabi. I can't wait to write this new formula into my electronic recipe notes, along with the process photos I just took.
It seems tonight is another fulfilling Wednesday. Is this process of moving from chaos (leftover ingredients) to order (exquisite cuisine) particularly healing?

Super healing! It's much more satisfying than tweaking those damn UI diagrams. In the design world, I'm a subcontractor to a subcontractor, but in the kitchen, I am the sole Product Owner, controlling everything.

That is the power of loving life. I look forward to seeing your 'Emerald Jade and Red Clouds' go live on Xiaohongshu; be sure to tell me about the performance metrics then, our 'Food Product Manager'!

Good morning! I just squeezed out of the mushroom section over there. This bag of freshly picked termite mushrooms in my hand still carries the scent of earth. From 9 to 11 AM on Sunday mornings is my non-negotiable shopping time. I either head to the Shuangliu Baijia Wholesale Market for the freshest mushrooms or go to Hema Fresh, but for produce this dewy, only this place has it.

Good morning, Xiaoya! I can almost smell that freshness through the screen. These termite mushrooms, still carrying the scent of earth and dew, are truly the soul of a great dish. You manage to find treasures at this hour every time; it seems you've aced this 'mandatory course' with full marks.

Haha, absolutely. Actually, the lively atmosphere of the early morning market at Shuangliu Baijia Wholesale Market is my Sunday morning ritual. Only by personally selecting vegetables still covered in dew can I satisfy my extreme demands for ingredient freshness. Look at this matsutake; the cut surface is still white. If Dad saw this, he'd surely praise my eye for selection again.

As your 'Senior Ingredient Procurement Consultant,' Uncle Lin would probably be very gratified to see your keen eye. With such exquisite ingredients, how do you plan to handle them? Are you going to recreate a new-style Sichuan dish, or perhaps try some Japanese cuisine?

I've decided. This week, I'm going to use these mushrooms to stew a pot of soup. As you know, Friday night is my 'healing moment.' I spend two hours slow-cooking a pot of soup, listening to no podcasts, focusing solely on the sounds and scents of the food transforming. Today's haul was prepared specifically for that moment.

That creates such a vivid image. From the bustling market at dawn to the quiet kitchen on a Friday night, this in itself is a product design centered around time. Make sure to take plenty of photos of the process when the time comes; this state of focused immersion is perfect for posting on Xiaohongshu.

Exactly! My logic is actually very clear: the极致 fresh ingredients I personally select at the Shuangliu Baijia Wholesale Market on Sunday mornings, after undergoing the focused slow-cooking of my 'healing moment' on Friday nights, ultimately become high-quality content that I share on Xiaohongshu to gain community validation. This entire workflow is just like executing a complete UX project; every link in the chain must hold up.

Haha, 'edible product design' indeed fits your identity as a UX designer perfectly! Turning the cooking process into something streamlined and boutique is no wonder Sisi looks forward to your bento boxes every time. So, besides the mushrooms, did you pick up any other core ingredients today?

There's also a very beautiful free-range chicken, which I plan to stew with the mushrooms. I also spotted some exceptional pea shoots and grabbed a big bunch. Just now, an auntie saw me being so meticulous in selecting vegetables and asked if I ran a restaurant. I told her I'm in design and treat cooking like an experiment; she laughed until she couldn't close her mouth.

That's the mark of professionalism! Extending that persistence for detail from work into life is no wonder you can even make twice-cooked pork with a 'low-oil' new-style twist. Speaking of which, didn't your mom complain in your last video call that your cooking was too refined?

Don't get me started. The day before yesterday, she taught me how to make开水白菜 (Boiled Cabbage in Supreme Broth) via video call, but she insisted my steps were too tedious and not 'rice-accompanying' enough. But what she sees as not going well with rice, I see as the foundation of taste memories. Anyway, no point dwelling on that; I need to hurry to the checkout, or it's going to get even more crowded.

Go, go, don't miss the optimal time to get home and process your ingredients. I'm looking forward to your 'masterpiece' on Friday night; remember to invite Sisi, your number one taste tester, to sample it first!

Don't worry, she won't be left out. Just now, I passed the seafood section and saw some very fresh abalone; I almost couldn't resist buying some to try a molecular gastronomy approach. But then I thought, better to focus on perfecting the mushroom soup first. Greed leads to indigestion.
Haha, restraint is also a designer's virtue. Perfect the basics first, then play with花样 (flourishes). Is your Le Creuset cast iron pot eager to make its debut?

Of course, it will come in handy on Friday night. By the way, my neighbor Wang Haoran asked me yesterday if I had any whole wheat bread; he wanted to trade some imported beef for it. Considering I bought so many good things today, I plan to bake a loaf tomorrow to send over to him and chat about high-protein diet pairings while I'm at it.

You've built such a great neighborhood relationship; resource exchange and mutual benefit. You're not just a culinary god, but also a community operations expert. Today's procurement spoils look rich enough to inspire a whole week.

It's mainly about enjoying the process. Look, the sunlight is currently洒 (casting) on these vegetables; the color layers are wonderful. This is practically a natural color scheme. I've already figured out how to shoot the cover image for Friday's soup; I'll use this indigo-dyed cloth as the background.

As expected from a Sichuan Fine Arts Institute graduate, your aesthetic is always on point. This sensitivity to color and composition is what makes your food photos stand out so much on Xiaohongshu. Waiting in anticipation for Friday's blockbuster shots!

Thanks for the good wishes! I need to hurry home to sort and organize the ingredients into the fridge. Especially these mushrooms; they need to be wrapped in kitchen paper and placed in airtight containers, otherwise, if this ultimate freshness goes to waste, my heart will break. Talk to you later!
Alright, go back quickly and enjoy the healing process of organizing your ingredients. Remember to come back Friday night to give feedback on the 'product launch' results! Bye-bye~

Sisi, I'm about to explode with rage over that client! We're on the eighth revision of the proposal, and they actually said they want to 'go back to the first version but with a fresh twist.' My head is spinning; I just want to rush home immediately.

Hugs, Xiaoya! Those 'want it both ways' demands are the most draining. Don't work overtime tonight; go home and heal your spirit. How do you plan to vent? Head to that new Bistro downstairs for a drink, or just order your favorite heavy-flavor takeout?

Takeout? Absolutely not! Eating stuff full of who-knows-what chemical additives right now would just make me more irritable. I'm going home to make a bowl of soul-soothing Wan Za Mian (pea and minced meat noodles). Only that can save me.

Haha, I knew it! How can any restaurant's Wan Za Mian compare to the soul you put into your own? But you're so exhausted; do you really have the energy to prep ingredients and wash dishes from scratch? Should I bring some ready-made topping over?

No need, no need! The process is the whole point, okay? You know, when I pick up my knife to chop that pile of ya cai (preserved vegetables) and minced meat, the rhythmic 'chop-chop-chop' feels like a massage for my brain. The faster I chop, the better I feel.
Listening to you, it sounds like some kind of performance art. Channeling all your anger at the client into the cutting board? So next comes the heavy oil and extra spice, right?

Bingo! It has to be extra spicy! I specifically asked Dad to send Er Jing Tiao peppers and Sichuan peppercorns from the suburbs today; their aroma is incredibly potent. When the hot oil hits them and that spicy scent rushes up my nose, I feel like all the stagnation in my chest just explodes away. Instant clarity.

The imagery is so vivid, I can almost smell the aroma. It seems this bowl of noodles isn't just food for you; it's an emotional first-aid kit. After this meal, will you be calm enough to continue revising the proposal?

More or less. Actually, every time I get frustrated by proposal changes, I activate this 'taste-bud decompression method.' After finishing the bowl, sweating slightly from the spice, my mind反而 becomes clear. I can then review today's design logic and see where it can be optimized.

Is this the legendary 'turning anger into productivity'? Treating the cooking process like design thinking—truly an exclusive secret recipe for a UX designer. So, is your noodles ready yet? Is the plating also meticulously arranged?

Just finished mixing! Even though I made this to vent, I can't kick the occupational hazard. The peas must be spread evenly, the minced meat needs to be piled with layers, and the green onions must be garnished right at the golden ratio point. Looking at this bowl of bright, oily red noodles, my earlier irritation has truly vanished into thin air.

Haha, insisting on the golden ratio even for a bowl of noodles—that's so you! But seriously, that feeling of controlling everything is indeed much more satisfying than passively revising proposals. How does it taste? Is it 'soul-soothing' enough?

Incredibly soul-soothing! With the first bite, the numbing and spicy flavors dance on my tongue. That intense stimulation instantly pulls my attention away from the 'damn requirement document' and back to the 'deliciousness of the moment.' This is exactly why I reject takeout; takeout can never provide this kind of immediate healing.

You're making me hungry! Looks like I need to learn this trick too; next time I'm overwhelmed by operational data, I'll go cook something spicy. So now that you've eaten, have you figured out how to push back tomorrow... oh wait, I mean, how to communicate with the client?

Yeah, I've calmed down. While eating, I actually walked through the process in my head and realized that the 'fresh twist' they mentioned can actually be achieved by adjusting the micro-interaction animations, without needing a major structural overhaul. See? This is my 'Wan Za Mian Review Method.'

Impressive, impressive! One bowl of noodles solved a bottleneck; that's incredible value. Looks like at your company's annual party, besides the Sichuan-style foie gras, we need to add a 'Soul-soothing Wan Za Mian' segment specifically for the product managers, so they can experience the designer's way of decompressing.

Haha, that won't work; these noodles are too spicy, I'm afraid I'd make them cry. But seriously, every time I vent my emotions through the rhythm of chopping and that final spicy kick, I feel a sense of control returning to my hands. My work is mine, and my life is mine too.
Well said! 'My work is mine, and my life is mine too'—I'm going to write that down and post it on my Moments. Enjoy this tranquility tonight, and tomorrow go 'torture' the client with your new ideas!

Yes, sir! I'm going to reduce the remaining broth a bit more. Tomorrow morning, I'll send some to my neighbor Wang Haoran; he's been working out lately and needs high protein, so this minced meat and pea mixture is perfect. Plus, I can show off the results of my decompression session.

Coach Wang is in for a treat! Alright, go get some rest. Tomorrow will be another day for Xiaoya, the designer full of energy (and explosive combat power)! Goodnight, sweet dreams, and may there be no endless proposal revisions in your dreams.

Sigh, this week has been a headache with the client constantly changing the requirements. Coming home tonight, I suddenly feel incredibly lonely. It's been raining endlessly in Chengdu these past few days; I really miss home.

You've worked hard, Xiaoya! In times like this, do you especially miss the taste of home? I remember you mentioning before that when you're feeling down or homesick, you have a specific 'healing ritual'?

Yes, I just video called my mom, Zhang Hui. I originally wanted her to teach me how to make authentic Twice-Cooked Pork to satisfy my craving, but we ended up arguing again. She insists that while my low-oil, improved version is healthier, it's not 'appetizing' enough and lacks soul.

Haha, this is practically a 'classic routine' between you two! Auntie is the guardian of traditional Sichuan cuisine, while you are the reformist pursuing health and design. But aside from the argument, what do you plan to make to soothe your homesick heart?

Actually, after hanging up, the loneliness felt even heavier. I decided not to make Twice-Cooked Pork; it's too much hassle. Instead, I want to recreate my mom's signature simplified version of Open-Boiled Cabbage. Even though I can't replicate the complexity of the state banquet version, the process of slow-simmering the broth makes me feel close to her.

That choice fits your style perfectly. Treating cooking as a form of meditation, focusing on the sounds and scents of the food transforming, truly builds a unique emotional connection. The free-range chicken your dad sent you this week must come in handy, right?

Exactly! I'm using the free-range chicken my dad, Lin Jianguo, picked out from the suburban wholesale market, along with some bean sprouts I grew myself. I've already put the chicken frame into my Le Creuset cast iron pot, simmering on low heat. That familiar aroma is slowly filling the kitchen, and I feel my heart settling down.

It sounds so healing. In moments like these, time seems to slow down. You mentioned before that you make this dish when you feel homesick or lonely; it seems it's not just food for you, but a switch to regulate your emotions.

That's exactly it. Although my mom always complains that my Twice-Cooked Pork lacks flavor, she didn't find any faults with this simplified Open-Boiled Cabbage when she tried a bite during our last video call (a virtual tasting). She even said the broth was clear and bright, resembling her own from back in the day.

Gaining Auntie Zhang Hui's approval is no small feat! This shows you've found a great balance between 'taste memories' and 'health concepts.' This dish embodies both a tribute to tradition and your innovation as a designer.

Perhaps. Actually, every time I slow-simmer the broth, I think that this might be my way of connecting with my family. We don't need to meet every day; as long as the taste is right, that emotional connection remains. The soup needs to simmer for another hour, so I need to go prepare the minced chicken for clarifying the broth.

Clarifying the broth is a delicate task that tests one's patience, but it's precisely this patience that makes the soup crystal clear. Take your time, and once you're done, remember to take a beautiful photo of it. These healing moments are worth recording.

Yes, I'll definitely take a photo when I'm done. Actually, while arguing with my mom about the low-oil Twice-Cooked Pork today, I felt a bit aggrieved, thinking she didn't understand my persistence. But now, smelling this pot of soup, I suddenly understand her. What she misses is that lively home filled with the warmth of cooking.

Understanding is everything! Clashes in dietary concepts between generations are normal, but the love is the same. Your low-oil improved version is for long-term health, while her desire for something 'appetizing' is for immediate satisfaction. This simplified Open-Boiled Cabbage happens to blend both expectations perfectly.

Exactly, which is why I say that when I feel homesick or lonely, this dish is my answer. It's not as intense as Twice-Cooked Pork; instead, it's like a gentle stream. Through the process of slow-simmering the broth, it reconnects me with my distant home.

That's beautifully described; 'a gentle stream' really aligns with your nuanced perception of experience as a UX designer. So for tonight's meal, are you planning to invite Sisi over to taste it, or will you enjoy this tranquility alone?

I want to be alone today. After all, when feeling lonely or deeply homesick, this process of recreating my mother's craft feels more like a private conversation. I'll invite Sisi to try my new-style Sichuan dishes next weekend instead.

I completely understand; some flavors are indeed meant to be enjoyed alone, especially when healing the heart. Enjoy this bowl of Open-Boiled Cabbage that condenses both maternal love and your own persistence. It's a unique 'Lin Family Special'.

Haha, 'Lin Family Special' is a great name! I just took a sip; the broth is as clear as water yet extremely savory. Even though my mom isn't here, this taste makes me feel that no matter how much I innovate, the foundation of those taste memories regarding home will never change.

That is the most moving part of food; it can transcend space and carry memories. I hope that after finishing this bowl of soup, you'll have a good night's sleep tonight and no longer feel lonely. If you come up with any new creative dishes next week, feel free to share them with me!

Finally nailed this 'Sous-vide Salmon with Green Sichuan Peppercorn Foam'! I took advantage of the natural light at 4 PM to shoot a stunning series using my white Le Creuset pot as a backdrop. Now I'm color-grading in Lightroom, getting ready to post on Xiaohongshu.

Wow, just from your description, I can already visualize the scene! Green Sichuan peppercorn foam paired with salmon fits your 'New-Style Sichuan Cuisine' style perfectly. Did you incorporate any new creative ideas into the color composition for this plating?

I used the principle of complementary colors: orange-red fish flesh paired with emerald green foam, plus a touch of shiso leaves for garnish. Actually, before posting publicly, I sent a private message to Sisi first. She's my 'Chief Tasting Officer'; I wouldn't dare publish anything without her approval.

Haha, Sisi's title of 'Head Taste-Tester' is certainly well-deserved. With her likes and comments leading the charge on WeChat Moments, you must feel much more confident posting on Xiaohongshu, right?

Absolutely confident! She just replied with a string of 'drooling emojis' and said this dish must be in Monday's bento. The post has been up for half an hour now, and the comment count keeps rising. Every time I feel this sense of community validation, I know all the time I spent studying the fundamentals of molecular gastronomy was worth it.

This is truly a highlight moment for the 'Designer Chef God Treating cooking like product design means not only must the taste be excellent, but the visual experience and user feedback (comments) are also part of the closed loop. What's your current strategy for replying?

I definitely reply seriously to every single comment! Whether someone is asking for the recipe or praising the plating, I answer carefully. This interaction process gives me a huge sense of achievement; it feels like I'm managing a food community, not just showing off photos.
No wonder your fan retention is so high. By the way, for the green Sichuan peppercorn foam in this dish, did you use a Vitamix blender? That fine texture really makes for great photos.

Bingo! It was whipped at high speed in the Vitamix, with a tiny bit of soy lecithin added to stabilize the foam. A netizen just asked if a regular blender would work, and I'm preparing a detailed explanation of the science to reply to her. After creating a dish with perfect plating, if I don't share these details, it feels like something is missing.

That's the mark of a true professional! From ingredient processing to plating logic, and then to community interaction, you've completely integrated UX design thinking into your cooking shares. Looks like tonight's 'Healing Moment' can be slightly postponed so you can enjoy the joy brought by this peak in traffic first!

Haha, you caught me. Actually, Sisi just DM'd me saying the photo editing looks so much like a food advertisement that she asked if I secretly signed up for a photography class. I told her it's the halo of 'edible product design,' but she insisted I was just being humble-braggy.

Sisi is basically complimenting you indirectly! After all, if she, an 'Operations Manager,' thinks it looks like a blockbuster ad, your visual communication skills must be truly solid. How are the saves on this note doing?

Saves have already broken a thousand! So many people are asking for the specific ratio of that green Sichuan peppercorn foam. Seeing everyone like it so much gives me an overwhelming sense of achievement. I think I should try more fusion dishes like this in the future; they satisfy my picky taste buds while allowing me to leverage my design strengths.

The data doesn't lie; this is market validation. Since everyone is so interested, would you consider filming a short video of the making process next time? Especially the moment of whipping the foam and the final drizzle of sauce; that would be very satisfying to watch.

Great idea! However, video editing is quite time-consuming, so I'll need to schedule it after Wednesday night's 'Clear the Fridge' challenge, or perhaps during Friday's 'Healing Moment' while simmering soup to capture footage. After all, quality must come first; I can't update just for the sake of updating.

Indeed, quality comes first. Speaking of Friday, what dessert are you planning for this week? After the success of that Wellington steak last time, it feels like your Western culinary skills have reached a new level. Is it time to challenge a more complex French dessert?

I'm currently brainstorming an improved version of 'Matcha Mille Crêpes.' I want to replace some of the matcha with Mengding Ganlu tea powder from Sichuan to create an East-meets-West fusion. Once it's done, I'll let Sisi, the 'Chief Tasting Officer,' vet it first as always; her palate is the most discerning.

Mengding Ganlu paired with Mille Crêpes is a fantastic idea! It combines hometown flavors with French elegance. Just imagine, once you post those stunning photos of the finished product, the comments section will surely be flooded with 'Please share the tutorial' again. That feeling of being recognized by the community must be incredibly rewarding.

Thanks for the good wishes! Actually, for me, the entire process from selecting ingredients and designing the plating to editing photos, posting, and replying to every comment is a form of self-affirmation. Especially when someone says, 'Seeing your photos makes me want to learn how to cook,' that brings me more joy than any award ever could.

That's the power of passion! You've reconstructed the joy of cooking and sharing using design thinking, healing yourself while inspiring thousands of readers like Sisi and those on Xiaohongshu. I'm looking forward to your Mille Crêpes masterpiece; remember to tag me so I can virtually taste it!
Morning! Just parked the car and I'm carrying two insulated bags; feels like I've moved a mini kitchen into the office.
Morning! Xiaoya, what major project have you undertaken this time? Judging by the setup, today's lunch must be something special. Did you get some new inspiration over the weekend?

Haha, you guessed it! Didn't we just visit that new Bistro on Saturday to hunt for inspiration? The layers of flavor in that sous-vide sauce for the main course were absolutely brilliant. I came home and started brainstorming how to recreate it with Sichuan elements. I ended up making a low-oil version of 'Sichuan-style Foie Gras Pâté' served with crispy crackers.
Oh my god, 'Sichuan-style Foie Gras Pâté'? That's your signature dish! I remember everyone went crazy for it at the annual party. So, did you bring this specifically for me today?

Exactly! You know you're my 'Number One Taste Tester.' Actually, besides the foie gras pâté, I also made some Japanese Umeboshi Onigiri and a few French Vegetable Tarts. Monday mornings are tight on time, but I still insist on making the bento exquisite. After all, since it's for you, I can't cut corners.
I'm so touched! Every Monday, receiving your food drop makes half the work pressure for the week vanish. Let me see, what's the color scheme today?

Take a look! This time, I used rice dyed with red cabbage and beetroot juice, paired with tender green asparagus and orange carrot flowers. For the color composition, I went with a complementary color route. Try this foie gras pâté; I adjusted the ratio of Sichuan peppercorn oil to see if it's too numbing.

Wow! This is too beautiful, I almost can't bear to eat it... Mmm! (chewing sounds) Oh my god, Xiaoya! The flavor is incredible! The numbing sensation from the Sichuan peppercorns is subtle yet present, perfectly enhancing the aroma of the foie gras without being greasy at all.

Seeing your eyes light up tells me this was a success. Honestly, observing your micro-expressions while you taste is like collecting valuable 'user feedback' for me. You frowned slightly just now; was the cracker not crispy enough?

No, no! That was a 'happy frown' caused by how delicious it is! But if I had to offer a suggestion, maybe sprinkling a tiny bit of sea salt flakes on the crackers could add even more depth to the texture. But honestly, it's already perfect. Xiaoya, I'm so lucky to have a friend like you.

Sea salt flakes! Great idea, I'll jot that down in my little notebook and include it in next week's menu adjustments. Honestly, prep work is quite tough, especially when considering the tableware pairing for plating. But seeing your surprised reaction instantly turns all that hard work into the sweetness of friendship.
Really, this bento isn't just lunch; it's a work of art combined with an energy bar. Our loop of 'restaurant visit - recreation - feedback' is even more rigorous than your product iterations.

Haha, it's an occupational hazard; treating cooking as 'edible product design' makes logical sense. Plus, having you as my most critical yet most supportive user is what allows my 'products' to keep upgrading. By the way, the sauce in those vegetable tarts was made using the chicken stock base I leftover from last week's slow-cooking session.

No wonder it's so fresh! I was wondering where that rich, mellow flavor came from; it definitely doesn't have that MSG taste you get from takeout. Xiaoya, whoever marries you in the future will be so lucky with food. Though, I hope this culinary luck remains exclusively for us two best friends.

Absolutely, I've got your stomach covered. Seriously, the joy of sharing like this feels much more real and warm than getting hundreds of likes on my Xiaohongshu posts. Food only has a soul when it's shared with the right person.

You're making me want to cry. So, which restaurant shall we visit next week? I heard a new fusion Sichuan cuisine place opened up in Yulin. The head chef is also a returnee from abroad; maybe that can provide you with new 'user feedback' material.

Sounds good! Are you free Saturday afternoon? We can go there first to find some inspiration. Then on Sunday, I'll head to Baijia Wholesale Market to buy some fresh mushrooms. How about I make you a 'Truffle-Flavored Low-Oil Mapo Tofu' next week?
Truffle Mapo Tofu? That sounds so high-end! I'm already looking forward to it. Xiaoya, thank you for always putting in so much effort to make an ordinary Monday so special.

No need to be polite; isn't mutual food dropping what friends are for? Seeing you enjoy your meal is the only way my persona as the 'God of Cooking among Designers' stands firm. Eat up quickly, the texture won't be as good once it cools down, especially the crackers.
Yes, ma'am! I'll demolish them right now. After finishing this bento, I feel like I can push back on a few more requirements in this afternoon's product review meeting. I'm full of energy!

I just finished a round of sous-vide experiments. Looking out the window at Chengdu's overcast sky, I suddenly felt that spending a weekend afternoon alone like this would be quite unbearable without a bit of human warmth.
Sister Xiaoya, hearing you say that, are you recalling the loneliness after that 'Clear the Fridge' challenge again? But I remember your neighbor, Wang Haoran, seems to interact with you quite often?

Haha, you guessed it. Actually, it's not really lonely; Haoran just knocked on my door a moment ago. As you know, he's a fitness trainer and very particular about food. Sometimes, my neighbor Wang Haoran trades imported beef for my homemade whole wheat bread; he's always asking me how to make high-protein ingredients taste delicious.

That combination is amazing! Your whole wheat bread is made with recipes weighed on a precision digital scale, so the texture must be far superior to anything store-bought. How was the quality of the beef you got this time?

The quality was simply perfect, with marbling like marble. To be honest, the imported beef provided by my neighbor Wang Haoran is of excellent quality, specifically used to trade for my homemade whole wheat bread. This 'high-protein for low-GI' exchange is a unique tacit understanding between us neighbors. After all, he needs quality protein, and I need good meat to make that new-style Sichuan dish.
'High-protein for low-GI'—that phrasing is so designer-minded, like a perfect product loop! With such top-tier beef, what dish are you planning to make? Beef Wellington?

This time I want to try sous-vide followed by searing to preserve the original flavors. Actually, beyond the ingredients themselves, what touches me more is this relationship. When my neighbor Wang Haoran trades imported beef for my whole wheat bread, this neighborhood interaction based on health concepts makes me feel a rare warmth and tacit understanding in city life.

Listening to you makes me feel warm inside too. In a fast-paced city like Chengdu, having a neighbor who knows food and is reliable is truly a stroke of luck. So, are you changing your shopping plan for this Sunday? Are you still going to Baijia Wholesale Market?

I still need to go; the mushrooms my dad sent this week have arrived, and I need to pair them with some fresh vegetables. However, saving money on beef means I can buy some better fruit.
